summary: Two personal letters from Iris Murdoch to Raymond Queneau (1949 and 1951) revealing her early academic struggles, her philosophical opposition to post-Wittgensteinian empiricism, and her ultimate belief that writing novels is her most important work.

principles.statement: Writing novels is the most important endeavor.
principles.rationale: Despite her active career and lecturing in philosophy, she views her literary work as her primary and most significant calling.
principles.source_quote: When I shall write a novel again I don't know and this after all is the only important thing.

principles.rationale: Even when interactions leave her feeling misunderstood or disconnected, she refuses to give up the effort to communicate with others.
principles.source_quote: However I don't resign myself to non-communication, here or anywhere.

signature_quotes.text: When I shall write a novel again I don't know and this after all is the only important thing.
signature_quotes.context: Reflecting on her academic workload in philosophy and her desire to return to literature.

signature_quotes.text: However I don't resign myself to non-communication, here or anywhere.
signature_quotes.context: Expressing her frustration with feeling unable to communicate properly with Queneau, but refusing to give up.

signature_quotes.text: If I could only stop thinking about marriage maybe I’d get some work done.
signature_quotes.context: On the distractions of her personal life.

anti_patterns.description: Accepting non-communication or giving up on connecting with others.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It leads to isolation; one must persist in trying to communicate even when previous attempts feel inadequate or sad.
She views it as a dominant but flawed trend in British philosophy, actively seeking to refute it in her thesis by drawing on Hegel and Sartre.
